driver.body: Payments is not a business Kepler runs for its own margin; it is the **plumbing that makes every other domain cash-free**. The rails are four concrete pieces of physical-world money movement — the ledger, the PSP and scheme connections, the KYC gate, and the settlement engine [S-050]. Run them slow, fragmented, or per-domain and the flywheel leaks: every domain rebuilds its own money-movement stack and the shared trust signal never accumulates in one place [S-050]. The strategic claim of Driver 1 is that **one ledger for all six domains is worth more than six optimised ledgers**, because the value is in the join, not in any single flow [S-050].

mechanisms.body:
Kepler runs **one double-entry ledger that every domain posts into**: a ride from D1, a charge from D2, a delivery payout to a D3 courier, a device instalment from D5 — all land as entries against the same account graph [S-051]. This is the mechanism that lets Payments see the whole customer. A rider who also couriers and leases a device is **one node with three revenue streams and one risk profile**, not three anonymous flows in three separate systems [S-051].

The design decision is recorded in trace S-401: the ledger is append-only, event-sourced, and the source of truth for both money movement and the behavioural features D6 later consumes [S-051]. That dual role is the load-bearing choice — the same write that moves money also emits a feature, so the system of record and the training-data source are one artefact rather than two that must be reconciled.

The nodes in the architecture converge in a fixed order, and each earns its place. The INGRESS API normalises every domain's payment intent into one schema, so six payment shapes become one. The RISK node calls D6 in-line against the fraud model on a ~40ms budget. The KYC/KYB GATE enforces tiered identity — tier 0 anonymous wallet top-up through tier 3 full underwriting-grade KYC — so the identity bar scales with the exposure. The **UNIFIED LEDGER is the join point** where all six domains finally meet in one account graph [S-051].

The FEATURE STORE bridge is what makes Payments a **contributor to the moat rather than a consumer of it**. Trace S-402 calls the feature-store write-back the single most under-valued line in the architecture — it is how a payment becomes training data, and therefore how Domain 4 feeds the D term of the moat instead of merely spending it [S-051].

No single scheme covers 14 countries economically, so Kepler **orchestrates across rails rather than committing to one** [S-052]. It routes across card schemes, local instant-payment rails — UPI in India, PIX in Brazil, PayNow in Singapore — and bank-transfer/RTP networks, sending each transaction to the **cheapest rail that clears the risk bar** [S-052].

The mechanism chains directly out of 1.1. Because the ledger is unified, the router makes a portfolio decision rather than a per-transaction one: it will accept slightly higher fraud on a low-margin D3 courier payout routed over a cheap local rail, because the same user's D1 ride history — fed in via the S-402 write-back — collateralises the risk [S-052]. The risk headroom comes from cross-domain data, not from anything intrinsic to the rail.

Trace S-403 captures the routing economics. Shifting **22 points of volume from card schemes to local rails cut blended cost-of-payment from 1.9% to 1.3% of TPV** — without raising realised fraud, because the tolerance was bought with data, not paid for in losses [S-052]. Six optimised ledgers could not make this trade: none of them would be holding the ride history that underwrites the courier payout, so each would price the local rail on its own thin flow and reject it.

Here Driver 1 hands off to the flywheel. Once the rails are unified and cheap, Kepler can make mobility and logistics **cash-free** — no driver handling notes, no courier reconciling coins [S-053]. This is the first cross-domain coupling, and it moves two different moat terms at once rather than one.

Removing cash raises D1's **interaction density (D)**, because more low-value rides become viable when there is no cash to fumble, and it raises D3's **network liquidity (L)**, because couriers get paid instantly and stay on the platform [S-053]. Trace S-404 measures both: in the six cities where cash was fully removed, **D1 ride frequency rose 11% and D3 courier churn fell 9pp** [S-053].

Neither number belongs to Payments' P&L, which is precisely the point. Payments spent margin to buy density and liquidity for other domains, and the moat identity M = D × B × L × T rose even though Domain 4's standalone contribution looked flat [S-053]. **A conglomerate scorecard would have killed this trade** — it would see a business burning margin on flows it does not book — while a flywheel scorecard sees two terms of the product lifting together, which is the only reading under which the spend makes sense [S-053].
